3 min read
4 hours ago

--

Every developer has experienced it.
You’re working on a React project, experimenting with new UI ideas, or asking an AI assistant to generate components.
Within seconds, you have a JSX file.
The code is ready.
Now you just want to see it.
But that’s where the real work begins.
A Simple Task That Became Repetitive
Previewing a JSX component sounds simple.
In reality, the process usually involves several unnecessary steps.
You open a React project.
Copy the component.
Install dependencies if needed.
Fix missing imports.
Run the development server.
Wait for everything to load.
Only then can you decide whether the component looks good.
When this happens once, it’s acceptable.
When it happens fifty times a week, it becomes frustrating.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The AI Revolution Changed Everything
AI tools have dramatically accelerated frontend development.
Instead of spending an hour building a component, developers can now generate one in less than a minute.
But while AI solved the problem of writing code faster, it didn’t solve the problem of reviewing that code.
In many cases, previewing the component became the slowest part of the workflow.
That felt backwards.
Asking a Different Question
Instead of asking,
“How can I make React development faster?”
I started asking,
“Why does previewing a JSX file require an entire React project?”
Images don’t.
PDFs don’t.
Markdown files don’t.
So why should JSX?
That simple question eventually led to PreviewKit.
What PreviewKit Tries to Do
PreviewKit isn’t another JavaScript framework.
It isn’t another code editor.
It doesn’t try to replace React, Vite, or Next.js.
Its purpose is much simpler.
Open a JSX file.
Render it.
Show the result.
That’s the experience I wanted.
One click.
One preview.
No unnecessary setup.
Designed Around Real Workflows
PreviewKit was built for the way developers actually work today.
We constantly receive components from different sources.
Sometimes from AI.
Sometimes from GitHub.
Sometimes from teammates.
Sometimes from old projects.
In every case, the first thing we want is visual feedback.
Not configuration.
Not installation.
Just the preview.
Why Small Improvements Matter
People often underestimate how much time repetitive tasks consume.
Saving one minute may not sound impressive.
But if you preview twenty or thirty components a day, those minutes quickly become hours over the course of a month.
Developer productivity isn’t always about writing code faster.
It’s often about removing the interruptions that break your flow.
Looking Forward
The future of software development will involve even more AI-generated code.
Developers will spend less time writing components and more time evaluating, refining, and improving them.
That means tools for reviewing code will become just as important as tools for generating it.
PreviewKit is built with that future in mind.
Not as a replacement for existing development tools, but as a companion that removes one small piece of friction from the workflow.
Because sometimes the best developer tool is the one that quietly saves you time, every single day.
